exemplar.title: MYP Personal Project - Art therapy - Mental Health of Teenagers

detailedExemplarData.title: MYP Personal Project - Art therapy - Mental Health of Teenagers

strengths: Comprehensive source-assessment table shows organized research and thoughtful credibility evaluation.
Detailed descriptions of information-literacy ATL skills with specific citations (American Art Therapy Association, Verywell Mind).
Demonstrated social-collaboration ATL skills through specialist interviews and school-leadership approvals.
Effective use of a mind-map diagram to organize and communicate art-therapy techniques, showcasing strong visual-communication skills.
weaknesses: Criteria used to assess source reliability (author credentials, publication date, peer review) are not described.
Research insights are not consistently linked to individual success criteria in the program design.
Feedback from specialist interviews is not shown to have directly informed specific program adjustments.
Mind-map branches lack annotations on therapeutic outcomes or connections to program objectives.
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: **Your application of ATL skills is thorough and well-documented**, especially in research organization, source citation, collaboration, and visual communication. **To deepen the impact,** clarify how you evaluated source credibility, explicitly link research findings to your success criteria, show how specialist feedback shaped program elements, and annotate your mind map with therapeutic outcomes to reinforce the connection between technique and objective.

strengths: Action plan is well-sequenced with monthly tasks and progress notes, demonstrating a feasible schedule.
Learning goal clearly articulates personal interest, grounding the project in an authentic connection between psychology and art therapy.
Rationale for selecting success criteria shows thoughtful prioritization of creativity, emotional well-being, and supportive environments.
Product goal defines a clear three-day art-therapy program aligned with the global context of Identities and Relationships.
weaknesses: Budget and delivery criteria remain generic; exact budget figures and timeline milestones are not specified.
Required resources (venue, art materials, expert contacts) are not detailed in the action plan.
Success criteria lack measurable, project-specific targets (e.g., participant satisfaction scores or completion rates).
Learning-goal benchmarks (number of case studies reviewed or interviews conducted) are not defined.
Methods for measuring each success criterion (survey ratings, observational checklists) are not clarified.
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: **Your planning demonstrates strong structure and authentic grounding.** The sequenced action plan and clear linkage of your learning goal to personal interest provide a solid foundation. You also show thoughtful prioritization in your success-criteria rationale and a well-defined product scope. **To enhance precision and accountability,** add exact budget figures, timeline milestones, required resources, and specific, measurable benchmarks and evaluation methods for each success criterion.

strengths: Product-evaluation table for Function offers balanced strengths and limitations, demonstrating critical self-assessment.
Reflection articulates self-growth in time management, feedback handling, and report structuring.
Credibility evaluation highlights professional insights and research depth, establishing a strong evidence-based foundation.
weaknesses: Reflections do not explicitly reference initial success criteria to show how each was achieved or requires improvement.
Evaluations of Environment, Budget, and Materials lack specific evidence (student feedback scores, cost reports) to validate assessments.
Expert input is limited; broadening to multiple specialists or peer reviews would diversify perspectives and strengthen credibility.
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: **Your reflections reveal thoughtful analysis of both the final product and your personal growth.** The critical evaluation of functionality and insights into learning strategies are strengths. **To strengthen your reflective practice further,** tie observations back to each success criterion, support your assessments of environment and resources with concrete data, and seek additional expert perspectives for a more rounded evaluation.

annotations.comment: Budget and delivery criteria remain genericâinclude exact budget figures and timeline milestones to ensure your plan is actionable and trackable.
Enhance the action plan by specifying required resources (e.g., venue, art materials, expert contacts) for each task to ensure completeness.
Make explicit connections between your reflections and the initial success criteria to demonstrate how each criterion was achieved or needs improvement.
Your product-evaluation table for Function includes balanced strengths and limitations, demonstrating critical self-assessment against your success criteria.
Your action plan is well sequenced, listing monthly tasks and progress notes, which demonstrates practical scheduling and feasibility.
Your source-assessment table is comprehensive, summarizing key information and evaluating credibility, which demonstrates organized research practice.
The success criteria table outlines general program elements but lacks project-specific, measurable targets (e.g., participant satisfaction scores, completion rates). Add these for clarity.
Your learning goal clearly articulates personal interest and shows a strong connection between psychology and art therapy, grounding the project in authentic experience.
Consider defining specific, measurable benchmarks (e.g., number of case studies reviewed or interviews conducted) to assess achievement of your learning goal.
You clearly detail how you applied information-literacy ATL skillsâciting specific sources (American Art Therapy Association, Verywell Mind) to inform program development.
To strengthen evidence of critical evaluation, describe the criteria you used to assess source reliability (e.g., author credentials, publication date, peer review).
Link each summarized insight directly to your programâs success criteriaâthis will clarify how research informs specific aspects of your design.
You demonstrate effective social-collaboration ATL skills by detailing interviews with a specialist and approvals from school leadership to refine your program.
Show how feedback from your specialist interview led to specific program adjustments (e.g., activity structure, participant selection) to illustrate impact on quality.
Your reflection articulates self-growth in time management, feedback handling, and report structuring, showing thoughtful analysis of personal learning.
Extend your evaluation of Environment, Budget, and Materials by citing specific evidence (e.g., student feedback scores, cost reports) to validate each assessment.
The credibility evaluation highlights professional insights and research depth, establishing a strong evidence-based foundation for your program.
Consider broadening your expert input (e.g., multiple specialists, peer reviews) to diversify perspectives and strengthen credibility further.
Your rationale for selecting success criteria shows thoughtful prioritization of creativity, emotional well-being, and support environments.
Clarify how you will measure each success criterion (e.g., survey ratings, observational checklists) to ensure precise, evidence-based evaluation.
Using a mind-map diagram effectively organizes and communicates the range of art-therapy techniques, showcasing strong visual-communication skills.
Annotate each branch of your mind map with brief notes on therapeutic outcomes to deepen analysis and link techniques to program objectives.
Strengthen planning by listing required resources (space, materials, personnel) and logistical details alongside your product description.
Your product goal clearly defines a three-day art-therapy program for MYP students and ties it effectively to the global context of Identities and Relationships.
